notable.notable_blurb: Choral director, WNYC host, founder of Canby Singers

cross_connections.display_label: a fellow descendant who composed and taught piano

narrative_blocks.header: WNYC radio host of recordings program for decades
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Tatnall Canby hosted a weekly recordings program on WNYC New York from 1946 to the mid-1970s — first called 'The New Recordings,' later known as 'Recordings, E.T.C.' He was based in the Saturday Review where he had a parallel record-review column. The program made him one of the most widely-heard American classical and electronic music critics of the postwar era. He was an early advocate of electronic music who brought listeners their first hearing of many experimental composers.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded the Canby Singers chamber choir 1950s
narrative_blocks.body: Canby founded the Canby Singers chamber choir in New York in the early 1950s — specializing in Renaissance and Baroque repertoire (Monteverdi, Byrd, Lassus, Josquin) but also performing twentieth-century works by Brahms, Hindemith, and Elliott Carter. The choir continued under his name and occasional guidance after his 1980s retirement. He had earlier joined ethnomusicologist Alan Lomax on 1930s tours collecting Appalachian folk music — making the first recordings of folk singer Jean Ritchie.

Canby's Audio magazine column ran for forty-eight years from 1948 to 1996 — one of the longest single-author runs in American magazine criticism. He fused his interest in musical performance with technical audio engineering, engineering all his WNYC programs himself on acetate-based tape long after polyester had become standard. He authored 'The Saturday Review Home Book of Recorded Music and Sound Reproduction' (1952); his Harvard music degree was earned in 1934.

notable.notable_blurb: Founder of Saturday Review; first editor Book of the Month Club

burial.plot_notes: Wilmington and Brandywine Cemetery, Wilmington DE

narrative_blocks.header: Founded the Saturday Review of Literature
narrative_blocks.body: Henry Seidel Canby was a leading figure in American literary criticism — founder of the Saturday Review of Literature, the influential weekly that became one of the principal venues of mid-twentieth-century American literary discourse. He served as a critical voice in the canonization of American literature across nearly half a century. His critical biographies focused on Mark Twain and Henry James, two of the central figures of the late-nineteenth-century American novel.

narrative_blocks.header: First editor-in-chief of Book of the Month Club
narrative_blocks.body: Canby was the founding editor-in-chief of the Book of the Month Club — one of the most influential mid-twentieth-century American institutions for shaping middlebrow literary taste and book purchasing. The Book of the Month Club's editorial selections under Canby helped define the American literary canon for general readers during the 1920s-1940s. Through this work he became one of the architects of twentieth-century American reading culture.

burial.plot_notes: Wilmington and Brandywine Cemetery, Wilmington DE

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.
